Career Advice 　>　 Getting Started 　>　 How to start looking for work

How to start looking for work

This section covers everything from job search dos and don’ts and what to include in your CV, as well as our key tips to help you how to find a new job, whether it’s full-time, part-time, or temp. Find out everything you need to know about looking for work now.

How to find a job to help you Love Mondays

Looking for a job you truly adore? We can help with that. Here’s your comprehensive guide to Loving Mondays.

Job search tips: How to find jobs online

Improve your job search results with practical tips on how to search for jobs online, use job sites effectively, and avoid common mistakes.

What job adverts really mean: How to decode the buzzwords

Unsure what dynamic or self-starter actually means? Decode the most common job advert buzzwords and phrases to boost your CV and find a job you’ll love.

What are the departments of a business?

A business isn’t one whole; it’s lots of parts working together. Here are some of the most common departments you might find.

Best part-time jobs for students

Looking for part-time jobs for students? Here are the best flexible roles, online jobs, and tips for balancing your studies with paid work.

How to complete your Reed.co.uk profile

Want to get noticed by top recruiters? Learn how to complete your Reed.co.uk profile, update your skills, and land your dream job faster.

Looking for jobs for 13-year-olds? Learn about the UK working age rules, working hour limits, and the best ways to earn your own money today.

What age can you start working?

Wondering what age you can start working? Find out about the legal minimum working age, maximum hours, and the best jobs for teenagers.

Learn how to spot employer red flags before accepting a job. From toxic workplace signs to interview red flags, here’s what jobseekers need to watch out for.

How to read a job description (and use it to apply)

Learn how to decode job descriptions, uncover what employers really want, and use those clues to write a stronger application.

What is affiliate marketing?

Curious about affiliate marketing? Find out what affiliate marketing is, how to choose the right affiliate programs, and how to get started.

How to research a company for an interview

Sometimes finding the best fit requires a little legwork. To help you get started, here are our top tips on how to research company.

11 ways you’re selling yourself short in your job search

Are you making common job search mistakes? Learn what to avoid in your job search, refine your applications, and find a role you love.

Top jobs of 2026: Most in-demand roles (and what they pay)

Here are the UK’s most in-demand jobs right now, from teaching and retail to tech and finance. Find out what they pay and how to land one.

How to find volunteer work

Looking for volunteer work? Our guide covers how to find volunteering jobs, your rights as a volunteer, and tips for teens.

Working abroad: How to get started

Thinking about working abroad? Here’s everything you need to know about visas, finding jobs abroad, and preparing for your finances.

How to find a temp job

Looking for a flexible position? Here’s how to find a temp job, the best roles to apply, and how to make yourself available to recruiters.

46 of the best entry-level jobs (and how to get them)

Need a job to get experience, but experience to get a job? Here are 46 entry-level roles (and our advice on how to get hired).

I need a job: where do I start?

Looking for work but don’t know where to begin? Whether you need a job now, need flexibility, or lack experience, here’s where to start.

How to decide on a career

Struggling to choose a career path? From assessing your skills to figuring out your working style, here’s how to decide on a career that’s right for you.

Job application forms: What you need to know

From job application tips to what to include, read our essential guide to acing your application. Also includes an example job application form.

What does competitive salary actually mean?

To help you decode one of the most commonly used job ad terms, here’s what competitive salary actually means (and why employers use it).

How to: Manage your mental health whilst jobseeking

To help you take the process one step at a time, here are some of our top tips on how to manage your mental health whilst looking for work.

next >

FAQs

How do I follow up after a job application if I haven’t heard back?

It’s tough waiting to hear back. Usually, it’s best to follow up a week or two after the application deadline. A short, polite email to the hiring manager or HR team works perfectly. For guidance and email examples, check our advice on how to follow up on a job application. Just reiterate your interest in the role and ask if there’s any update on your application.

How do I start looking for a job?

Starting your job hunt is all about getting organised. Firstly, do some research into what you actually want from a job – think about the industry, role, and salary you’re after, along with your ideal working environment (whether it’s remote, hybrid, or on-site). Once you’ve got a clear idea, you can use job boards and recruitment agencies to find your perfect match, and set up job alerts to have opportunities sent directly to you. Then, you can check out how to write a CV and use our free CV template to make your application stand out. 

What are the most important skills employers are looking for?

This will depend on the role you’re applying for; so check the job description to find out what skills should be highlighted on your CV and cover letter. Generally though, employers are looking for a mix of both hard and soft skills. Hard skills are technical abilities you can learn, like data analysis or Excel wizardry. Soft skills are all about your personality, including communication, teamwork, and problem-solving – key qualities that help you excel in any workplace. 

How can networking help my job search?

Networking is one of the most effective ways to find out about opportunities and get your foot in the door. Start by reaching out to friends, family, former colleagues, and even university contacts. Attend industry events, join online groups, and don’t be shy about connecting with people on LinkedIn. Remember, networking isn’t just about asking for a job. Share your goals and interests, offer your own insights, and you’ll build relationships that can lead to useful tips, referrals, and sometimes even job offers you won’t find on job boards.
